digitalTwin.title: Brewing Digital Twin
digitalTwin.description: Interactive brewing simulation — adjust recipe parameters and see quality score in real time.
digitalTwin.grindHelp: Use one of the 5 common grind levels so settings are easier to compare across different grinder models.
digitalTwin.calibrationHint: Use your grinder-specific click conversion as a reference and pick the matching grind level.
digitalTwin.simulatedQualityScoreDescription: Higher scores indicate a more stable and reproducible brewing result.
digitalTwin.scoreFeedbackHigh: Very high reproducibility is expected. This recipe is likely to unlock the bean's full potential with an ideal extraction balance.
digitalTwin.scoreFeedbackMid: A good extraction is expected. Small adjustments (water temperature or grind size) may improve clarity even further.
digitalTwin.scoreFeedbackLow: There is a risk of over-extraction (harshness) or under-extraction (overly sharp acidity). Review your brewing recipe balance.

glossary.title: Coffee Glossary
glossary.description: Learn common coffee terms to brew with confidence
glossary.noResults: No matching terms were found. Try another keyword.

acidity.short: A bright, lively sensation often similar to citrus or apple.
acidity.explanation: In coffee, acidity means pleasant brightness, not harsh sourness. Good acidity makes flavor feel clear and juicy. It is often more noticeable in light roasts and some origins such as Ethiopia or Kenya.
acidity.tip: If acidity feels too sharp, try a slightly finer grind or hotter water.

body.short: The weight and texture of coffee in your mouth.
body.explanation: Body describes mouthfeel: light and tea-like, smooth and medium, or heavy and syrupy. It is different from strength or caffeine. Brewing method and roast level strongly affect body.
body.tip: French press usually gives fuller body than paper-filter pour-over.

bloom.short: The bubbling phase right after first pour in manual brewing.
bloom.explanation: When hot water first touches fresh grounds, trapped CO₂ escapes and creates bubbles. This stage is called bloom. A short bloom period can improve even extraction in pour-over brewing.
bloom.tip: Try 30–45 seconds bloom before continuing your main pours.

brewRatio.short: The relationship between coffee dose and water amount.
brewRatio.explanation: Brew ratio is usually written like 1:15 or 1:16, meaning 1g coffee for 15–16g water. It is one of the easiest ways to tune cup strength and balance.
brewRatio.tip: A common starting point for pour-over is around 1:15 to 1:16.

cupping.short: A standardized method to taste and compare coffee.
cupping.explanation: Cupping is a professional tasting protocol used to evaluate aroma, flavor, acidity, body, and aftertaste. It helps compare lots consistently and communicate quality objectively.
cupping.tip: Think of cupping as a shared language for coffee quality.

extraction.short: How much flavor is dissolved from grounds into water.
extraction.explanation: Extraction is controlled by grind size, temperature, brew time, and agitation. Under-extraction often tastes sour and thin, while over-extraction can taste bitter and dry.
extraction.tip: Change one variable at a time when dialing in.

extractionYield.short: The percentage of coffee mass dissolved into the cup.
extractionYield.explanation: Extraction yield estimates how much soluble material moved from grounds to beverage. For filter coffee, many brewers target roughly 18–22% for balanced flavor.
extractionYield.tip: If cups are consistently harsh, your extraction may be too high.

grindSize.short: How coarse or fine your ground coffee is.
grindSize.explanation: Finer grounds extract faster because they have more surface area. Coarser grounds extract slower. Matching grind size to brew method is key for repeatable results.
grindSize.tip: Pour-over often starts around medium to medium-fine.

tds.short: Total Dissolved Solids: concentration of dissolved coffee in the drink.
tds.explanation: TDS indicates how strong your brewed coffee is, often measured by refractometer. Higher TDS usually means stronger concentration, while lower TDS tastes lighter.
tds.tip: TDS shows strength, not necessarily quality by itself.

underOver.short: Two common extraction imbalance patterns in brewing.
underOver.explanation: Under-extracted cups can taste sour, salty, or watery. Over-extracted cups can taste bitter, harsh, or drying. Adjusting grind, time, and temperature helps rebalance.
underOver.tip: Sour-thin often needs more extraction; bitter-dry often needs less.

roasting.short: The process of heating green coffee beans to develop coffee flavor and aroma.
roasting.explanation: Roasting transforms green beans through controlled heat and time. As the roast progresses, flavor moves from bright and acidic to sweeter, then darker and more bitter. This is why coffees are commonly grouped into light, medium, and dark roast levels.
roasting.tip: Roast level has a major impact on acidity, sweetness, body, and bitterness.

flavorNotes.short: Words used to describe coffee aroma and taste by comparing them to familiar foods.
flavorNotes.explanation: Flavor notes are sensory descriptors such as berry, citrus, nuts, chocolate, or floral tones. They help communicate what a coffee tastes and smells like without adding those ingredients. Notes can change depending on roast level, brewing method, and extraction quality.
flavorNotes.tip: Use flavor notes as a guide, not a strict rule—your perception can differ.

arabica.short: The dominant coffee species worldwide, known for balanced sweetness, acidity, and complex aroma.
arabica.explanation: Arabica accounts for roughly 60–70% of global coffee production. It is generally more delicate and climate-sensitive than Robusta, which makes cultivation harder. Most specialty coffees belong to Arabica because of its nuanced and often floral or fruity flavor profile.
arabica.tip: Many famous varieties, including Geisha and Typica, belong to Arabica.

robusta.short: A hardy coffee species with strong bitterness, roasty character, and high caffeine.
robusta.explanation: Robusta (Coffea canephora) is the second most-produced coffee species after Arabica. It resists pests better and grows well in lower altitudes, making it easier to cultivate. It often has heavier body, stronger bitterness, and around twice the caffeine of Arabica, so it is common in espresso blends and instant coffee.
robusta.tip: Robusta can add crema, strength, and punch to espresso blends.

geisha.short: A premium Arabica variety known for floral aroma and citrus-like acidity.
geisha.explanation: Geisha is celebrated for jasmine-like florals, tea-like clarity, and elegant citrus notes. It often scores highly in competitions and can be expensive due to limited yield and high demand. When roasted and brewed carefully, it can show exceptional complexity.
geisha.tip: Use gentle brewing and lighter roast styles to preserve Geisha’s delicate aromatics.

typica.short: One of the oldest Arabica lineages, valued for clean cup quality.
typica.explanation: Typica is a historic Arabica variety group that influenced many later cultivars. It is known for clarity and refined sweetness, but it tends to be lower-yield and more disease-sensitive. That combination makes high-quality Typica coffees relatively rare.
typica.tip: Typica is foundational in coffee history and breeding.

bourbon.short: An Arabica variety descended from Typica, often sweet and rounded in cup profile.
bourbon.explanation: Bourbon is widely appreciated for rich sweetness, balanced acidity, and full flavor. Compared with Typica, it can show different plant and bean characteristics depending on region and mutation. Many high-quality Latin American coffees include Bourbon lineage.
bourbon.tip: Bourbon lots often show caramel-like sweetness and smooth texture.

caturra.short: A natural mutation of Bourbon with compact plant size and productive growth.
caturra.explanation: Caturra became popular because its shorter tree height can improve farm management and yield. In cup quality, it can present bright acidity and crisp character depending on terroir and processing. It is widely cultivated in Latin America.
caturra.tip: Lower tree height can make harvesting and farm maintenance easier.

pacamara.short: A hybrid of Pacas and Maragogipe, famous for very large beans and complex flavor.
pacamara.explanation: Pacamara is known for oversized bean size and distinctive cup profiles, often combining vivid acidity with heavy body and layered aromatics. It is grown notably in Central America and can taste very expressive when processed and roasted well.
pacamara.tip: Pacamara can be bold and complex—great for cupping and experimental brews.
